comments:
Summary:
This paper studies elementary loops in logic programs, reformulates the notion for nondisjunctive programs, extends it to disjunctive programs, and explores properties and applications. The authors define elementarily unfounded sets, introduce the class of Head-Elementary-loop-Free (HEF) programs (a strict generalization of Head-Cycle-Free programs), and prove a number of technical results: characterization theorems relating elementary loops and loop formulas, polynomial-time recognition of elementary loops for nondisjunctive programs (via an "elementary subgraph"), coNP-completeness of recognizing elementary loops in general disjunctive programs, equivalence of HEF programs and their shifted (nondisjunctive) variants, extensions of inherent tightness results to HEF, and operational/stability-checking results (bounding loops and their use). The work relates elementary loops closely to minimal unfounded sets and discusses implications for SAT-based ASP solvers.

Strengths:
- Substantial theoretical contribution: the paper systematically develops the notion of elementary loops beyond prior work (Gebser & Schaub) and extends many classical theorems (Lin & Zhao, Lee, etc.) to a broader setting.
- Novel class (HEF) that strictly generalizes HCF, with useful semantic properties (shifting preserves stable models, polynomial-time stability checking) and clear complexity results delineating tractable vs intractable cases.
- Careful connections established between elementary loops, elementarily unfounded sets, and minimal unfounded sets; the characterization results are likely to be of interest to the ASP semantics community.
- Reasonably clear presentation of main ideas; multiple illustrative examples help in understanding definitions and edge cases.

Weaknesses / Suggestions for improvement:
- Practical impact and empirical validation are not addressed. The paper motivates potential improvements for SAT-based ASP solvers by focusing on elementary loops, but does not provide experimental evidence or concrete heuristics showing that restricting to elementary loops yields performance gains in practice. A small empirical section or at least more detailed discussion of how the theoretical results would be integrated into solvers would strengthen the paper.
- Some algorithms are described at a high level (e.g., computing bounding loops, or constructing elementary subgraphs). It would be helpful to provide explicit pseudocode, complexity analysis, and implementation remarks so that the results are easier to adopt in tools.
- Although proofs appear rigorous in outline, many are sketched; the paper would benefit from either fuller proofs or a clear appendix pointer for all technical details (if this is a conference-length version, that is understandable). A short roadmap of which proofs are in the body vs deferred would improve readability.
- The PDF extraction provided here contained repeated fragments and some formatting glitches (likely not the authors’ fault). The final version should ensure the presentation is clean and that definitions/theorems are numbered and cross-referenced consistently.

Minor comments:
- The relationship to the Gebser & Schaub definition is discussed and clarified — this is valuable. It might help readers to have a short table or figure summarizing which properties hold for Loops vs Elementary Loops vs Elementarily Unfounded Sets.
- The paper mentions potential future work (lifting to first-order, further subclasses where HEF testing is tractable); more concrete open problems or conjectures would be useful.

Overall assessment:
This is a technically solid and original contribution to the theory of answer set programming and the study of loop formulas/unfounded sets. It extends important semantic results to a broader class (HEF), clarifies relationships among several semantic notions, and establishes meaningful complexity boundaries. I recommend acceptance; the paper would be stronger with more practical guidance, pseudocode for key constructions, and (ideally) some experimental evidence of the utility of focusing on elementary loops in solvers.
